subtasks.testStrategy: Test each toggle for correct on/off state persistence. Verify keyboard navigation (Tab, Space/Enter). Check ARIA labels with a screen reader. Confirm visual consistency with the design system in both light and dark modes.

subtasks.title: /unauthorized Error Page with P.I.T Branding
subtasks.description: Redesign the /unauthorized (403) error page to include P.I.T branding, a clear error message, and a call-to-action to redirect users appropriately.
subtasks.details: 1. Create or update the /unauthorized page component. 2. Add the P.I.T logo/branding consistent with the auth pages (Task 32) and design system. 3. Display a clear, user-friendly 403 Unauthorized message with a brief explanation. 4. Add a primary CTA button (e.g., 'Go to Home' or 'Log In') using design system button tokens. 5. Apply background pattern or subtle texture consistent with auth pages. 6. Use semantic design tokens for all colors, spacing, and typography. 7. Ensure the page is fully responsive and accessible.
subtasks.testStrategy: Manually navigate to /unauthorized and verify branding, message clarity, and CTA functionality. Test responsiveness on mobile and desktop. Confirm design token usage and visual consistency with other branded pages.

subtasks.title: 404 Error Page Redesign with P.I.T Branding
subtasks.description: Redesign the 404 Not Found error page with P.I.T branding, a friendly error message, and navigation options to guide users back to valid content.
subtasks.details: 1. Create or update the 404 page component (e.g., app/not-found.tsx or pages/404.tsx). 2. Integrate P.I.T logo and branding elements consistent with the design system. 3. Display a clear '404 – Page Not Found' heading with a helpful, friendly description. 4. Add CTA buttons (e.g., 'Back to Home', 'Go to Dashboard') using design system tokens. 5. Apply consistent background, typography, and color tokens. 6. Optionally include a subtle illustration or icon to enhance the visual appeal. 7. Ensure full responsiveness and accessibility compliance.
subtasks.testStrategy: Navigate to a non-existent route to trigger the 404 page. Verify branding, message, and CTA links. Test on mobile and desktop viewports. Confirm all design tokens are applied correctly and no hardcoded colors remain.

subtasks.title: 500 Error Page Redesign with P.I.T Branding and Support Info
subtasks.description: Redesign the 500 Internal Server Error page with P.I.T branding, a clear error message, and support contact information to assist users experiencing server-side issues.
subtasks.details: 1. Create or update the 500 error page component (e.g., app/error.tsx or pages/500.tsx). 2. Integrate P.I.T logo and branding consistent with the design system and other error pages. 3. Display a clear '500 – Something Went Wrong' heading with a user-friendly explanation. 4. Include support contact information (e.g., support email, link to help center) so users know how to get help. 5. Add a CTA button (e.g., 'Try Again', 'Back to Home') using design system button tokens. 6. Apply consistent background, typography, spacing, and color tokens. 7. Ensure the page is fully responsive and accessible. 8. Align visual style with the 404 page for consistency across all error pages.
subtasks.testStrategy: Trigger a 500 error in a test environment to verify the page renders correctly. Check that branding, error message, support info, and CTA are all present and functional. Test responsiveness and confirm design token usage. Compare visually with the 404 page for consistency.

tasks.expansionPrompt: Break utility pages redesign into subtasks: (1) Payment pages hardcoded color removal and token application, (2) /settings/notifications with toggle components, (3) /unauthorized error page with P.I.T branding, (4) 404 error page with branding, (5) 500 error page with branding and support info.

tasks.title: SEO, OG Images and Social Engine
tasks.description: Dynamic Open Graph for player profiles, tournaments, teams. Sitemap XML for public pages. JSON-LD for tournaments with dates. Dynamic meta tags via Next.js metadata API. robots.txt configuration.

subtasks.title: Dynamic Open Graph Meta Tags for Player Profiles
subtasks.description: Implement dynamic Open Graph meta tags for player profile pages using the Next.js metadata API, including OG images, titles, and descriptions tailored to each player.
subtasks.details: In the /app/players/[id] or equivalent route, use Next.js `generateMetadata` async function to fetch player data and return dynamic `openGraph` metadata fields: `title`, `description`, `url`, `images` (with player avatar or a dynamically generated OG image via Next.js `/api/og` route using `@vercel/og` or `next/og`). Include `twitter:card`, `twitter:title`, `twitter:description`, and `twitter:image` tags. Fallback to default values if player data is unavailable. Ensure canonical URLs are set correctly.
subtasks.testStrategy: Verify OG tags render correctly by inspecting page HTML for player profile routes. Use tools like opengraph.xyz or Facebook Sharing Debugger to validate tags. Test fallback behavior when player data is missing.

subtasks.title: Dynamic Open Graph Meta Tags for Tournaments
subtasks.description: Implement dynamic Open Graph meta tags for tournament detail and list pages using the Next.js metadata API, including tournament-specific OG images, titles, and descriptions.
subtasks.details: In the /app/tournaments/[id] route, use Next.js `generateMetadata` to fetch tournament data and return dynamic `openGraph` fields: `title` (e.g., 'Tournament Name | Platform'), `description` (tournament summary, dates, prize pool), `url`, and `images` (tournament banner or dynamically generated OG image). Also add `twitter:card` meta tags. For the /tournaments list page, use static metadata with a general description. Reuse the OG image generation pattern established in subtask 1.
subtasks.testStrategy: Validate OG tags on tournament detail pages using opengraph.xyz. Confirm dynamic data (name, dates) appears correctly in meta tags. Test with missing tournament data to verify fallbacks.

subtasks.title: Dynamic Open Graph Meta Tags for Teams
subtasks.description: Implement dynamic Open Graph meta tags for team pages using the Next.js metadata API, including team-specific OG images, titles, and descriptions.
subtasks.details: In the /app/teams/[id] or equivalent route, use Next.js `generateMetadata` to fetch team data and return dynamic `openGraph` fields: `title` (e.g., 'Team Name | Platform'), `description` (team bio, member count, stats), `url`, and `images` (team logo or dynamically generated OG image). Include `twitter:card` meta tags. Reuse the OG image generation utility from subtask 1. Ensure team list page has appropriate static metadata. Handle cases where team data is unavailable with sensible defaults.
subtasks.testStrategy: Inspect HTML of team pages for correct OG tag values. Use social media debugger tools to preview link unfurls. Test fallback metadata when team ID does not exist.

subtasks.title: Sitemap XML Generation and robots.txt Configuration
subtasks.description: Generate a dynamic sitemap.xml covering all public pages (players, tournaments, teams, static routes) and configure robots.txt to guide search engine crawlers.
subtasks.details: Create /app/sitemap.ts using Next.js built-in sitemap support (returning a `MetadataRoute.Sitemap` array). Include static routes (home, /tournaments, /teams, /players) and dynamically fetch all public entity IDs to generate entries for /tournaments/[id], /teams/[id], /players/[id] with `lastModified`, `changeFrequency`, and `priority` fields. Create /app/robots.ts using `MetadataRoute.Robots` to define `User-agent: *` rules, disallow private/auth routes (e.g., /profile, /api, /admin), and set the `Sitemap` URL pointing to the generated sitemap.xml. Ensure both files are accessible at /sitemap.xml and /robots.txt.
subtasks.testStrategy: Access /sitemap.xml and /robots.txt in the browser and validate structure. Use Google Search Console or an XML sitemap validator to check sitemap format. Verify disallowed routes are not indexed by inspecting robots.txt rules.

subtasks.title: JSON-LD Structured Data for Tournaments
subtasks.description: Implement JSON-LD structured data on tournament pages to provide rich search engine results with event dates, location, and organizer information.
subtasks.details: In the /app/tournaments/[id] page component (server component), inject a `<script type='application/ld+json'>` tag with a JSON-LD `Event` schema (schema.org/Event). Include fields: `name`, `startDate`, `endDate` (ISO 8601 format), `description`, `url`, `organizer` (platform name), `location` (online event with `@type: VirtualLocation`), and optionally `offers` for prize pool info. Use Next.js `<Script>` component with `strategy='beforeInteractive'` or inline via dangerouslySetInnerHTML in a server component. Validate the schema using Google's Rich Results Test. Also add `SportsEvent` type if applicable for better categorization.
subtasks.testStrategy: Use Google's Rich Results Test (search.google.com/test/rich-results) to validate JSON-LD output for tournament pages. Confirm all required Event schema fields are present and correctly formatted. Test with tournaments that have and without end dates.

tasks.expansionPrompt: Structure SEO implementation into subtasks: (1) Dynamic Open Graph meta tags for player profiles, (2) Dynamic Open Graph meta tags for tournaments, (3) Dynamic Open Graph meta tags for teams, (4) Sitemap XML generation for public pages, (5) JSON-LD structured data for tournaments with dates, robots.txt configuration. Use Next.js metadata API.

tasks.title: Performance Budget and Optimization
tasks.description: Define JS budget per critical page. Validate lazy-load in admin/moderation. Optimize Recharts and large tables with virtualization. Lighthouse audit for landing and profile (target: 90+ performance, 90+ accessibility). Remove heavy dependencies. Analyze bundle with next/bundle-analyzer.

subtasks.title: Define JS Performance Budget per Critical Page
subtasks.description: Establish JavaScript size budgets for each critical page (landing, profile, team) to set measurable performance targets and enforce limits during CI/CD.
subtasks.details: Audit current JS bundle sizes for landing, profile, and team pages using Next.js build output. Define per-page JS budgets (e.g., landing ≤ 150KB, profile ≤ 200KB, team ≤ 180KB compressed). Document budgets in a `performance-budget.json` file. Integrate budget checks into CI pipeline using tools like `bundlesize` or Next.js built-in size limits in `next.config.js`. Set up alerts when budgets are exceeded. Include both initial load and total JS size targets.
subtasks.testStrategy: Verify CI pipeline fails when bundle sizes exceed defined budgets. Run `next build` and confirm size report matches documented targets. Review budget file for completeness across all three critical pages.

subtasks.title: Validate and Enforce Lazy-Loading in Admin/Moderation Pages
subtasks.description: Audit and validate that all heavy components and routes in admin and moderation pages are properly lazy-loaded using dynamic imports and code splitting.
subtasks.details: Review all imports in `/admin` and `/moderation` route files. Replace static imports of heavy components (charts, tables, modals, editors) with `next/dynamic` or React `lazy()` + `Suspense`. Validate that code splitting is working correctly by inspecting network tab — chunks should load on demand, not upfront. Add loading skeletons or spinners as fallback UI during lazy load. Specifically check ClaimQueue, DisputeViewer, TournamentManager, and UserManagement components (from Task 43). Measure initial JS payload reduction before and after.
subtasks.testStrategy: Use Chrome DevTools Network tab to confirm admin/moderation route chunks are loaded lazily. Verify no heavy component bundles appear in the initial page load. Write integration tests that assert Suspense fallback renders before component resolves.

subtasks.title: Optimize Recharts for Large Datasets
subtasks.description: Implement performance optimizations for Recharts components when rendering large datasets, including data downsampling, memoization, and lazy rendering strategies.
subtasks.details: Identify all Recharts usage across the codebase. For large datasets (>500 data points), implement data downsampling/aggregation before passing to chart components. Wrap chart components with `React.memo` and use `useMemo` for expensive data transformations. Consider replacing heavy Recharts components with lighter alternatives (e.g., `recharts` ResponsiveContainer with reduced tick counts). Implement lazy rendering for charts not in the viewport using Intersection Observer. Evaluate if specific Recharts modules can be tree-shaken by importing directly (e.g., `recharts/es6/component/...`). Measure render time before and after optimizations.
subtasks.testStrategy: Benchmark chart render time with 1000+ data points before and after optimization using React DevTools Profiler. Confirm no unnecessary re-renders via memo checks. Validate charts still display correctly with downsampled data.

subtasks.title: Implement Virtualization for Large Tables
subtasks.description: Add row virtualization to large data tables throughout the application to prevent DOM bloat and improve rendering performance for lists with hundreds or thousands of rows.
subtasks.details: Identify all table components rendering large datasets (e.g., UserManagement, tournament lists, leaderboards). Integrate `@tanstack/react-virtual` or `react-window` for row virtualization. Refactor existing table components to support virtualized rendering while maintaining sort, filter, and selection functionality. Set a threshold (e.g., >50 rows) to automatically switch to virtualized mode. Ensure accessibility is maintained (ARIA roles, keyboard navigation) in virtualized tables. Measure DOM node count and scroll performance before and after. Update Storybook/component gallery entries if applicable.
subtasks.testStrategy: Measure DOM node count with 500-row table before and after virtualization — expect >80% reduction. Test scroll performance with Chrome DevTools Performance tab targeting 60fps. Verify keyboard navigation and screen reader compatibility with virtualized tables.

subtasks.title: Lighthouse Audit for Landing and Profile Pages (Target: 90+ Performance & Accessibility)
subtasks.description: Run comprehensive Lighthouse audits on landing and profile pages, identify bottlenecks, and iteratively optimize until achieving 90+ scores in both Performance and Accessibility categories.
subtasks.details: Run baseline Lighthouse audits (CI and manual) on landing and profile pages in production-like environment. Document current scores across Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices, and SEO. Address top Performance issues: LCP optimization (image preloading, font loading), CLS fixes (explicit dimensions on images/embeds), FID/INP improvements (defer non-critical JS). Address Accessibility issues: color contrast ratios, missing ARIA labels, focus management, keyboard navigation. Use `lighthouse-ci` for automated auditing in CI pipeline with score thresholds. Iterate until both pages consistently score 90+ on Performance and Accessibility.
subtasks.testStrategy: Run `lighthouse-ci` in CI pipeline and assert Performance ≥ 90 and Accessibility ≥ 90 for both landing and profile pages. Manually verify with Chrome Lighthouse in incognito mode. Document before/after scores in a performance report.

subtasks.title: Bundle Analysis and Heavy Dependency Removal with next/bundle-analyzer
subtasks.description: Analyze the production bundle using next/bundle-analyzer to identify oversized or redundant dependencies, then remove or replace heavy packages to reduce overall bundle size.
subtasks.details: Install and configure `@next/bundle-analyzer` in `next.config.js` with `ANALYZE=true` flag. Generate bundle visualization for both client and server bundles. Identify the top 10 largest dependencies and evaluate each for: (a) necessity, (b) lighter alternatives, (c) tree-shaking opportunities. Common targets: moment.js → date-fns, lodash → lodash-es or native, large icon libraries → selective imports. Remove unused dependencies from `package.json`. Replace heavy dependencies with lighter alternatives where feasible. Re-run bundle analysis to confirm size reductions. Update JS budget file (Subtask 1) with new baselines after cleanup.
subtasks.testStrategy: Compare bundle analyzer reports before and after cleanup — target ≥ 20% reduction in total JS size. Run full test suite to ensure no regressions from dependency replacements. Verify CI budget checks pass with updated thresholds.

tasks.expansionPrompt: Decompose performance optimization into subtasks: (1) Define JS budget per critical page (landing, profile, team), (2) Lazy-load validation in admin/moderation pages, (3) Recharts optimization for large datasets, (4) Large table virtualization implementation, (5) Lighthouse audit for landing and profile (target 90+ performance/accessibility), (6) Bundle analysis with next/bundle-analyzer and heavy dependency removal.

tasks.title: Visual Regression Tests and Component Gallery
tasks.description: Create /dev/components route (dev only) to visualize all shared components with states (default, loading, disabled, hover, error, empty). Screenshot tests for critical pages (profile, team, tournaments, landing). Before/after comparison to protect design system.

subtasks.title: Create /dev/components Route (Dev-Only Component Gallery)
subtasks.description: Set up a protected /dev/components route that is only accessible in development mode, serving as the entry point for the component gallery.
subtasks.details: 1. Create `src/app/dev/components/page.tsx` with a guard that returns 404 or redirects in production (`if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'development') notFound()`). 2. Add a layout `src/app/dev/layout.tsx` with a dev-only banner/header. 3. Create a sidebar or tab navigation listing all shared components (Button, Card, Modal, Table, Sidebar, Navbar, etc.) sourced from `src/components/shared/`. 4. Add a route group or middleware check to block the route in non-dev environments. 5. Ensure the route is excluded from the production build sitemap and robots.txt. 6. Add a README note documenting the gallery URL and purpose.
subtasks.testStrategy: Manually verify the route returns 404 in a production build (`NODE_ENV=production`). In development, confirm navigation renders and all shared component links are listed. Run `next build` to ensure no dev-only pages leak into the production bundle.

subtasks.title: Implement Component State Showcase (default, loading, disabled, hover, error, empty)
subtasks.description: For each shared component in the gallery, render all required states side-by-side: default, loading, disabled, hover, error, and empty.
subtasks.details: 1. Create a `ComponentShowcase` wrapper component (`src/app/dev/components/_components/ComponentShowcase.tsx`) that accepts a component and renders it in a labeled grid for each state. 2. Define a `StateVariant` type: `'default' | 'loading' | 'disabled' | 'hover' | 'error' | 'empty'`. 3. For each shared component (Button, Card, Modal, Table, Sidebar, Navbar, Badge, Input, etc.), create a dedicated showcase file (e.g., `ButtonShowcase.tsx`) that passes the appropriate props/state to trigger each variant. 4. Use CSS `:hover` simulation via `data-state='hover'` or Storybook-like forced state props where native hover cannot be captured in a static render. 5. Label each state clearly with the variant name above the rendered component. 6. Import and register all showcases in the `/dev/components` page with anchor links.
subtasks.testStrategy: Visually inspect each component in the browser at `/dev/components` and confirm all 6 states render without errors. Write a smoke test using React Testing Library to assert each state label is present in the DOM for at least one component (e.g., Button).

subtasks.title: Screenshot Tests for Critical Pages (profile, team, tournaments, landing)
subtasks.description: Set up automated screenshot (visual snapshot) tests for the four critical application pages using Playwright or a similar tool.
subtasks.details: 1. Install Playwright (`npm install -D @playwright/test`) and initialize config (`playwright.config.ts`) targeting `localhost:3000`. 2. Create `tests/visual/` directory with test files: `landing.spec.ts`, `profile.spec.ts`, `team.spec.ts`, `tournaments.spec.ts`. 3. Each spec should: navigate to the page, wait for network idle, take a full-page screenshot with `page.screenshot({ fullPage: true })`, and compare against a stored baseline using `expect(screenshot).toMatchSnapshot()`. 4. Configure viewports for desktop (1440px), tablet (768px), and mobile (375px) in each test. 5. Store baseline snapshots in `tests/visual/__snapshots__/` and commit them to the repository. 6. Add an npm script `test:visual` to `package.json`. 7. Handle authentication/mock data for profile and team pages using Playwright fixtures or MSW.
subtasks.testStrategy: Run `npm run test:visual` locally to generate initial baselines. Intentionally change a CSS variable (e.g., primary color) and confirm the test fails with a diff. Restore the change and confirm tests pass again.

subtasks.title: Before/After Comparison Setup for Design System Protection
subtasks.description: Establish a baseline management workflow and visual diff reporting system so that design system changes are caught and reviewed before merging.
subtasks.details: 1. Configure Playwright's `toMatchSnapshot` with a pixel-difference threshold (e.g., `{ maxDiffPixelRatio: 0.01 }`) to allow minor anti-aliasing differences. 2. Integrate `playwright-visual-regression` or use Playwright's built-in snapshot update command (`--update-snapshots`) for intentional baseline updates. 3. Set up an HTML diff report using `playwright-report` (already built-in) that shows side-by-side before/after images on failure. 4. Create a `scripts/update-visual-baselines.sh` script that runs tests with `--update-snapshots` and commits the new baselines with a conventional commit message. 5. Document the baseline update process in `docs/visual-regression.md`: when to update, how to review diffs, and approval process. 6. Add `.gitattributes` rules to treat snapshot PNG files as binary to avoid merge conflicts.
subtasks.testStrategy: Simulate a design system change (modify a CSS token), run tests, confirm the HTML report shows a clear before/after diff image. Run the update script, confirm new baselines are committed, and verify tests pass with the updated snapshots.

subtasks.title: CI/CD Integration for Automated Visual Regression Testing
subtasks.description: Integrate visual regression tests into the CI/CD pipeline so they run automatically on pull requests and block merges when visual regressions are detected.
subtasks.details: 1. Create `.github/workflows/visual-regression.yml` (or equivalent for the project's CI provider) that triggers on `pull_request` events targeting `main`/`develop`. 2. Workflow steps: checkout code → install dependencies (`npm ci`) → install Playwright browsers (`npx playwright install --with-deps`) → start the dev server (`npm run dev &`) → wait for server readiness (`npx wait-on http://localhost:3000`) → run `npm run test:visual`. 3. Upload Playwright HTML report as a CI artifact on failure (`actions/upload-artifact`) so reviewers can download and inspect diffs. 4. Cache Playwright browser binaries between runs using `actions/cache` keyed on the Playwright version. 5. Add a branch protection rule requiring the `visual-regression` CI check to pass before merging. 6. Configure the workflow to allow manual baseline updates via a `workflow_dispatch` trigger with an `update-baselines` input flag that runs `--update-snapshots` and commits back to the branch.
subtasks.testStrategy: Open a test PR with a deliberate CSS change, confirm the CI workflow triggers, fails, and uploads a diff artifact. Merge a PR with no visual changes and confirm the workflow passes. Test the `workflow_dispatch` baseline update path by triggering it manually and verifying the snapshot commit appears on the branch.

tasks.expansionPrompt: Structure visual regression testing into subtasks: (1) Create /dev/components route (dev-only) for component gallery, (2) Implement component state showcase (default, loading, disabled, hover, error, empty), (3) Screenshot tests for critical pages (profile, team, tournaments, landing), (4) Before/after comparison setup for design system protection, (5) CI/CD integration for automated visual regression testing.
